intro.title: Introduction to Data Structures
intro.content:
[b]What Is Data?[/b]
[i]Data[/i] refers to raw facts and values that represent information. These can be numbers, text, symbols, or measurements. Examples include a student’s age, a name, a test score, or a true or false value.

[b]Data Types[/b]
In programming, data is classified into [i]data types[/i]. Common data types include integers, floating point numbers, characters, strings, and boolean values. Data types tell the computer how much memory to use and what operations are allowed.

[b]Basic Operations on Data[/b]
Programs perform operations such as creating data, reading data, updating values, and deleting data. These operations are essential for any software system.

[b]Why Do We Need to Organize Data?[/b]
Programs handle large amounts of data such as records, messages, scores, and transactions. If data is not organized properly, programs become slow, difficult to maintain, and prone to errors.

[b]What Is a Data Structure?[/b]
A [i]data structure[/i] is a method of storing and organizing data in memory so it can be accessed and modified efficiently. It defines how data is stored, how it is accessed, and how operations are performed.

[b]Real-Life Analogy[/b]
Think of data like books. If books are scattered randomly, finding one is difficult. When books are arranged on shelves by category, searching becomes easier. A data structure works like this shelf system.

[b]Importance of Data Structures[/b]
Data structures help programmers write faster programs, use memory efficiently, and manage large amounts of data. Many systems such as databases, search engines, games, and navigation apps rely heavily on data structures.

[b]Summary[/b]
Data is raw information. Data types define how data is used. Data structures organize data efficiently and make programs easier to understand and maintain.

array.title: Arrays
array.content:
[b]What Is an Array?[/b]
An [i]array[/i] is a data structure that stores multiple elements of the same data type in contiguous memory locations.

[b]Array Representation[/b]
Each element in an array is accessed using an index. Indexing usually starts at zero, meaning the first element is at index zero.

[b]Basic Operations[/b]
Common array operations include traversal, insertion, deletion, searching, and updating elements.

[b]Insertion[/b]
Insertion means adding a new element at a specific position. Existing elements may need to shift to make space.

[b]Deletion[/b]
Deletion removes an element from a given index. Remaining elements shift to fill the gap.

[b]Searching[/b]
Searching involves finding the position of a specific value in the array.

[b]Updating[/b]
Updating replaces the value stored at a specific index.

[b]Summary[/b]
Arrays are simple and fast for accessing elements but have fixed size limitations.

linked_list.title: Linked List
linked_list.content:
[b]What Is a Linked List?[/b]
A [i]linked list[/i] is a data structure where elements are stored as nodes. Each node contains data and a reference to the next node.

[b]Representation[/b]
Unlike arrays, linked lists do not store elements in contiguous memory.

[b]Types of Linked Lists[/b]
Common types include singly linked lists, doubly linked lists, and circular linked lists.

[b]Basic Operations[/b]
Operations include insertion, deletion, traversal, and searching.

[b]Summary[/b]
Linked lists allow dynamic memory usage but require more memory for references.

doubly_linked_list.title: Doubly Linked List
doubly_linked_list.content:
[b]What Is a Doubly Linked List?[/b]
A [i]doubly linked list[/i] is a linked list where each node has references to both the next and previous nodes.

[b]Advantages[/b]
It allows traversal in both forward and backward directions.

[b]Disadvantages[/b]
It uses more memory due to extra pointers.

[b]Summary[/b]
Doubly linked lists provide flexibility at the cost of additional memory usage.

circular_linked_list.title: Circular Linked List
circular_linked_list.content:
[b]What Is a Circular Linked List?[/b]
A [i]circular linked list[/i] is a linked list where the last node points back to the first node.

[b]Key Feature[/b]
There is no null reference at the end of the list.

[b]Use Cases[/b]
Circular linked lists are useful in scheduling and continuous data processing.

[b]Summary[/b]
This structure allows efficient looping through data.

stack.title: Stacks
stack.content:
[b]What Is a Stack?[/b]
A [i]stack[/i] is a linear data structure that follows the Last In, First Out principle.

[b]Basic Operations[/b]
Push adds an element to the top. Pop removes the top element. Peek views the top element.

[b]Real-Life Example[/b]
A stack of plates is a common analogy.

[b]Summary[/b]
Stacks are widely used in function calls, undo operations, and expression evaluation.

queue.title: Queues
queue.content:
[b]What Is a Queue?[/b]
A [i]queue[/i] is a linear data structure that follows the First In, First Out principle.

[b]Basic Operations[/b]
Enqueue adds an element to the rear. Dequeue removes an element from the front.

[b]Real-Life Example[/b]
People lining up in a queue follow the same rule.

[b]Summary[/b]
Queues are useful in scheduling, buffering, and task management.

tree.title: Trees
tree.content:
[b]What Is a Tree?[/b]
A [i]tree[/i] is a hierarchical data structure consisting of nodes connected by edges.

[b]Binary Search Tree[/b]
A [i]binary search tree[/i] stores data such that the left subtree contains smaller values and the right subtree contains larger values.

[b]BST Operations[/b]
Common operations include insertion, deletion, and searching.

[b]Tree Traversals[/b]
Traversal methods include inorder, preorder, and postorder.

[b]Graph Traversals[/b]
Breadth First Search and Depth First Search are used to explore tree structures.

[b]Summary[/b]
Trees are powerful for representing hierarchical relationships.

algo_intro_search.title: Introduction to Algorithms
algo_intro_search.content:
[b]What Is an Algorithm?[/b]
An [i]algorithm[/i] is a step by step procedure for solving a problem.

[b]Searching Algorithms[/b]
Searching algorithms are used to locate a specific element in a data structure.

[b]Importance[/b]
Efficient algorithms reduce execution time and resource usage.

[b]Summary[/b]
Understanding algorithms is essential for problem solving in computer science.

linear_search.title: Linear Search
linear_search.content:
[b]Linear Search[/b]
Linear search checks each element one by one until the target value is found or the list ends.

[b]Characteristics[/b]
It is simple but inefficient for large datasets.

[b]Summary[/b]
Linear search works on both sorted and unsorted data.

binary_search.title: Binary Search
binary_search.content:
[b]Binary Search[/b]
Binary search repeatedly divides a sorted list in half to locate a value.

[b]Requirement[/b]
The data must be sorted.

[b]Summary[/b]
Binary search is faster than linear search.

interpolation_search.title: Interpolation Search
interpolation_search.content:
[b]Interpolation Search[/b]
Interpolation search estimates the position of the target based on value distribution.

[b]Best Case[/b]
It works best on uniformly distributed data.

[b]Summary[/b]
This search can be faster than binary search in specific cases.

hash_table.title: Hash Table
hash_table.content:
[b]What Is a Hash Table?[/b]
A [i]hash table[/i] stores data using key value pairs.

[b]Hash Function[/b]
A hash function converts a key into an index.

[b]Summary[/b]
Hash tables provide very fast data access.

algo_intro_sort.title: Introduction to Sorting Algorithms
algo_intro_sort.content:
[b]Sorting Algorithms[/b]
Sorting algorithms arrange data in a specific order such as ascending or descending.

[b]Why Sorting Matters[/b]
Sorting improves searching efficiency and data organization.

[b]Summary[/b]
Different sorting methods suit different scenarios.

bubble_sort.title: Bubble Sort
bubble_sort.content:
[b]Bubble Sort[/b]
Bubble sort repeatedly swaps adjacent elements if they are in the wrong order.

[b]Summary[/b]
It is easy to understand but inefficient for large datasets.

insertion_sort.title: Insertion Sort
insertion_sort.content:
[b]Insertion Sort[/b]
Insertion sort builds a sorted list one element at a time.

[b]Summary[/b]
It works well for small or nearly sorted data.

selection_sort.title: Selection Sort
selection_sort.content:
[b]Selection Sort[/b]
Selection sort repeatedly selects the smallest element and places it in the correct position.

[b]Summary[/b]
It is simple but not very efficient.

merge_sort.title: Merge Sort
merge_sort.content:
[b]Merge Sort[/b]
Merge sort divides data into smaller parts, sorts them, and merges them back together.

[b]Summary[/b]
It is efficient and uses the divide and conquer approach.

quick_sort.title: Quick Sort
quick_sort.content:
[b]Quick Sort[/b]
Quick sort selects a pivot and partitions data around it.

[b]Summary[/b]
It is very fast on average but performance depends on pivot selection.

shell_sort.title: Shell Sort
shell_sort.content:
[b]Shell Sort[/b]
Shell sort improves insertion sort by allowing comparisons over larger gaps.

[b]Summary[/b]
It is faster than simple insertion sort for large datasets.
